items.title: Classification of hemorrhoids by grade
items.contentHtml:
Classification of hemorrhoids by grade

  • Grade I: they protrude into the anal canal without prolapse; painless bleeding. Grade II: they prolapse with defecation but reduce spontaneously. Grade III: they prolapse and require manual reduction. Grade IV: permanent irreducible prolapse; They may be associated with thrombosis or strangulation. The classification is clinical, not endoscopic; The treatment varies according to the grade: elastic band ligation indicated in grades I-III.

items.foco: External hemorrhoidal thrombosis: diagnosis and management
items.title: External hemorrhoidal thrombosis: diagnosis and management
items.contentHtml:
External hemorrhoidal thrombosis: diagnosis and management

  • It presents as a painful nodule of acute onset on the anal margin; A firm, bluish tumor is palpable. Differential diagnosis: perianal abscess (more extensive inflammatory signs, fever). Management: if < 72 hours and severe pain → surgical excision (thrombectomy) under local anesthesia; if > 72 hours or mild symptoms → conservative management: NSAIDs, sitz baths, laxatives. Elastic band ligation is not indicated for external hemorrhoids.

items.title: Perianal fistula: Goodsall's rule
items.contentHtml:
Perianal fistula: Goodsall's rule

  • The Goodsall's rule predicts the fistulous path: external orifice anterior to the transverse line of the anus → rectilinear path towards the corresponding crypt; posterior external osm → curved path towards the posterior midline. Exception: anterior orifices > 3 cm from the anal margin usually have a curved path. Useful for planning surgery (fistulotomy vs fistulectomy). Trap: the rule fails in fistulas secondary to Crohn's disease or previous abscesses.

items.title: Perianal abscess: urgent surgical drainage
items.contentHtml:
Perianal abscess: urgent surgical drainage

  • Perianal abscess (ischiorectal, intersphincteric, supralevator) requires urgent surgical drainage to avoid sepsis. It is not recommended to wait for it to fluctuate; The diagnosis is clinical (pain, erythema, induration, fever). Technique: radial incision near the anal margin, drainage, culture if immunocompromised. Do not use antibiotics as a sole treatment; only adjuvants if extensive cellulitis, valvular disease or immunosuppression. Up to 50% progress to perianal fistula.

items.title: Anal fissure: hypertonic internal sphincter
items.contentHtml:
Anal fissure: hypertonic internal sphincter

  • The primary anal fissure is associated with hypertonic internal sphincter due to muscle contracture that reduces blood flow to the fissured area, perpetuating the injury. The pain is intense during and after defecation, with bright red bleeding. First-line medical treatment: Topical nitroglycerin 0.2-0.4% or topical nifedipine. If it fails, internal lateral sphincterotomy (surgical) is considered. Trap: lateral or atypical anal fissure requires ruling out Crohn's disease, HIV or anal cancer.

items.foco: Anal fissure: medical treatment with nitroglycerin
items.title: Anal fissure: medical treatment with nitroglycerin
items.contentHtml:
Anal fissure: medical treatment with nitroglycerin

  • Topical nitroglycerin 0.2% (ointment) applied every 12 hours for 6-8 weeks is first line for chronic anal fissure. Mechanism: donates nitric oxide, relaxes the hypertonic internal sphincter and improves perfusion. Efficacy ~50-70%; Adverse effect: headache (up to 30%). Contraindication: use of sildenafil or tadalafil (risk of severe hypotension). Alternative: topical nifedipine 0.2% or topical diltiazem 2%. Surgery is reserved if medical failure.

items.title: Crohn's disease: complex perianal fistulas
items.contentHtml:
Crohn's disease: complex perianal fistulas

  • Complex perianal fistulas in Crohn are high, multiple tracts, with associated branches or abscesses. Management: control of the inflammatory disease with biological therapy (anti-TNF: infliximab, adalimumab) combined with surgical drainage (seton). Do not perform fistulotomy due to high risk of incontinence. Goodsall's rule has low precision in Crohn's. Image: Pelvic MRI is the gold standard for classifying paths (Parks classification).

items.foco: Anal cancer: relationship with HPV and HIV
items.title: Anal cancer: relationship with HPV and HIV
items.contentHtml:
Anal cancer: relationship with HPV and HIV

  • Anal cancer (mainly squamous cell carcinoma) is associated with HPV infection (types 16 and 18) and HIV, especially in men who have sex with men. HIV accelerates the progression of anal intraepithelial neoplasia (AIN). Diagnosis: biopsy of suspicious lesion (mass, ulcer, bleeding). Treatment: Concurrent chemoradiotherapy (mitomycin + 5-FU + radiotherapy) is standard; surgery (abdominoperineal resection) reserved for relapses. Trap: not to be confused with hemorrhoids; Anal cancer lesions are usually indurated and painful.

items.title: Perianal suppurative hidradenitis: differential diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Perianal suppurative hidradenitis: differential diagnosis

  • Perianal suppurative hidradenitis is a chronic inflammatory disease of the apocrine glands, with nodules, abscesses and fistulas in skin folds (armpits, groin, perianal region). Differential diagnosis: cryptoglandular perianal fistula (internal orifice in a dentate line, single tract), Crohn's disease (complex fistulas, intestinal involvement). Key: in hidradenitis there is no communication with the anal canal or internal orifice; The lesions are bilateral and with comedones. Treatment: antibiotics (clindamycin-rifampicin), anti-TNF, extensive surgery.

items.title: Anal itching: most common causes
items.contentHtml:
Anal itching: most common causes

  • Anal itching Idiopathic is the most common (up to 50%). Secondary causes: contact dermatitis (soaps, wet wipes, topical anesthetics), infections (candidiasis, pinworms, dermatophytes), anal diseases (hemorrhoids, fissure, fistula), inverted psoriasis, Crohn's disease. Management: Gentle hygiene, avoid irritants, treat underlying cause. Trap: do not use high-potency topical corticosteroids for a long time (risk of skin atrophy).

items.title: Pilonidal: Limberg surgery or marsupialization
items.contentHtml:
Pilonidal: Limberg surgery or marsupialization

  • The pilonidal sinus is a cavity with hairs in the sacrococcygeal region. Limberg surgery (rhomboid flap) offers a lower recurrence rate (<5%) than marsupialization (opening the cavity and suturing the edges to the skin). Acute management: pilonidal abscess → incision and drainage; later elective surgery. Risk factors: obesity, sedentary lifestyle, hirsutism. Trap: marsupialization allows healing by secondary intention, but with a longer healing time than Limberg.

items.title: Hemorrhoid management: elastic band ligation
items.contentHtml:
Management of hemorrhoids: elastic band ligation

  • Elastic band ligation is the outpatient treatment of choice for grade II and grade III internal hemorrhoids. A band is placed at the base of the hemorrhoidal bundle above the dentate line (prevents severe pain). Complications: pain (if the band is below the dentate line), bleeding (up to 7-10 days post-procedure), external hemorrhoidal thrombosis (rare), pelvic sepsis (very rare but lethal; suspect if severe pain and fever). Contraindicated in immunocompromised patients.
